topic: A musician hears their song being performed by someone else
raw_output:
[story 1]

The silence in the small bar was suddenly suffocating. I gripped the edge of the worn wooden table, my knuckles turning white. My melody, the one I’d poured six months of sleepless nights into, was spilling out from the stage, perfectly executed, but it wasn't mine. It was someone else’s interpretation, polished and somehow richer. I watched the performer—a young man with effortless stage presence—and felt a cold knot tighten in my stomach. Every note that flowed from his fingers felt like a direct dismissal of my own fragile creation. When the final chord faded, the applause washed over him, a warm, enveloping wave, while I just sat there, the phantom echo of my music stinging my ears. I slowly gathered my things, the movement stiff and jerky, desperate to escape the spotlight that suddenly felt too hot to bear.

[story 2]

I stood by the back wall, pretending to examine a dusty bottle of water, but my gaze was locked on the band. They were playing my song. Every strum, every vocal run, was a mirror reflecting my deepest fears of inadequacy. A flush crept up my neck, spreading across my cheeks until they felt burning. I wanted to bolt, to vanish into the shadows of the venue, but my feet seemed rooted to the floor. When the singer hit the bridge—that soaring, complex section I’d labored over—the sound seemed to mock my own efforts. I clenched my jaw so hard I tasted copper. I didn't dare look up, focusing instead on the scuff marks on the floor, trying to physically shrink myself, to become invisible amidst the vibrant noise.

[story 3]

The air in the rehearsal space seemed to thicken, turning heavy and viscous. I leaned against the doorframe, trying to breathe shallowly, hoping the sheer proximity to the sound wouldn't shatter me. When the band launched into my piece, it was technically flawless, undeniably powerful, and utterly devoid of the raw, aching vulnerability I had intended. I could feel a distinct tightening in my chest, a pressure building behind my ribs that made it hard to draw a full breath. I watched the lead guitarist lean back, basking in the audience’s reaction, and a wave of profound disappointment washed over me. I took a shaky step back, my gaze fixed on the floorboards, wishing the ground would swallow me whole.
